AAPL.text:
Operator: Good afternoon, and welcome to the Apple Inc. Fourth Fiscal Quarter 2024 Earnings Conference Call. I would now like to turn the call over to Tim Cook, Chief Executive Officer.

Tim Cook, CEO: Thank you, and good afternoon, everyone. We are pleased to report revenue of $94.9 billion for the September quarter, up 6 percent year over year and an all-time record for the quarter. We set revenue records in a number of markets, including the US, Canada, Western Europe, and several emerging markets. iPhone revenue came in at $46.2 billion, up 5.5 percent year over year, driven by strong demand for iPhone 16 and iPhone 16 Pro. We saw particularly robust adoption of the Pro models, which benefited from the introduction of Apple Intelligence features. In Greater China, revenue was $15.0 billion, down approximately 2 percent on a constant currency basis. We continue to face a competitive environment there, but our installed base remains strong and customer satisfaction scores are at record levels.

Our Services business delivered another outstanding quarter with revenue of $25.0 billion, up 12 percent year over year and a new all-time record. We now have over 1.1 billion paid subscriptions across our services portfolio. Mac revenue was $7.7 billion, down modestly from the prior year as customers awaited the transition to M4 silicon. iPad revenue was $6.9 billion, reflecting the successful launch of iPad Pro with M4 and iPad Air with M3. Wearables, Home, and Accessories generated $9.0 billion.

Luca Maestri, CFO: Thank you, Tim. Gross margin was 46.2 percent for the quarter, up 60 basis points year over year, reflecting a favorable mix shift toward Services and cost efficiencies in our supply chain. Products gross margin was 36.3 percent, while Services gross margin reached 74.0 percent. Operating expenses were $14.3 billion, reflecting continued investment in R&D, particularly in AI and machine learning capabilities. We generated $27.0 billion in operating cash flow during the quarter and returned over $29 billion to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases.

Looking ahead, for the December quarter, we expect total revenue to grow in the low-to-mid single digits on a year-over-year basis. We expect gross margin to be between 46.0 and 47.0 percent. We anticipate Services will continue its double-digit growth trajectory, though we expect some headwinds in Greater China. OpEx is expected to be between $15.3 and $15.5 billion as we ramp investment in Apple Intelligence.

Operator: We will now begin the question-and-answer session.

Analyst, Morgan Stanley: Tim, could you provide more color on the adoption of Apple Intelligence features? Are you seeing this drive upgrade cycles, and how should we think about the monetization opportunity over time?

Tim Cook: We are really excited about the reception to Apple Intelligence. The features we launched in October are being widely adopted, and we are seeing a meaningful increase in engagement on devices that support it. While it is still early to quantify the direct impact on upgrade rates, we are encouraged by the mix shift toward Pro models, which offer the full Apple Intelligence experience. We believe this is a multi-year tailwind for both hardware upgrades and services engagement. We have a very aggressive roadmap for 2025 that we think customers will love.

Analyst, Goldman Sachs: Luca, can you walk us through the puts and takes on gross margin? Given the mix shift toward services, is there a path to sustainably higher margins, or are there offsets we should be aware of?

Luca Maestri: Gross margin performance has been strong, and Services mix is certainly a tailwind. However, I would caution that we also face headwinds from foreign exchange, particularly the weakening of several currencies against the dollar. Additionally, as we ramp new products and invest in AI infrastructure, there can be near-term cost pressures. We are managing these carefully, but I would not extrapolate a linear margin expansion from here. We guide quarter to quarter for good reason.

Analyst, Bank of America: Tim, on China specifically, can you characterize the competitive dynamics you are seeing with Huawei and other domestic players? Is market share stabilizing?

Tim Cook: China remains an incredibly important market for us. The competitive landscape is intense, and we respect all of our competitors there. What I would point to is our customer satisfaction and loyalty metrics, which remain best in class. We saw improvement in urban tier-one cities during the quarter, and the iPhone 16 launch response was encouraging. However, we are not immune to the broader macroeconomic pressures in the region, and the regulatory environment continues to evolve. We are investing for the long term in China and remain optimistic about our position.

JPM.text:
Operator: Good morning, and welcome to JPMorgan Chase Third Quarter 2024 Earnings Call. I would now like to turn the call over to Jamie Dimon, Chairman and CEO.

Jamie Dimon, CEO: Thank you, and good morning. The firm reported third quarter net income of $12.9 billion, or $4.37 per share, on revenue of $42.6 billion. Return on tangible common equity was 19 percent. These results reflect the resilience of our diversified business model, though I want to be clear that the environment remains uncertain. Geopolitical risks are elevated, inflation is proving stickier than many expected, and while the consumer remains in decent shape, we are beginning to see normalization in credit performance that warrants vigilance.

Consumer and Community Banking generated revenue of $17.8 billion, driven by strong deposit margins and continued growth in card services. Average deposits were down 1 percent year over year as rate-sensitive customers moved balances into higher-yielding products, but we are seeing stabilization. Card loans grew 11 percent year over year, and we continue to gain market share in card originations.

The Corporate and Investment Bank delivered revenue of $17.0 billion. Investment banking fees were $2.2 billion, up 31 percent year over year, as capital markets activity continues to recover from the depressed levels of 2022 and 2023. We ranked number one in global IB fees for the quarter. Markets revenue was $7.2 billion, with Fixed Income at $4.5 billion and Equities at $2.7 billion, both showing solid performance.

Jeremy Barnum, CFO: Thank you, Jamie. Net interest income was $23.4 billion for the quarter, up 3 percent year over year but down 1 percent sequentially as we began to feel the impact of anticipated rate cuts. We expect NII excluding Markets to be approximately $91.5 billion for the full year 2024, which reflects our updated rate path assumptions. For 2025, we would guide to NII ex-Markets in the range of $87 to $88 billion, reflecting the full-year impact of lower rates.

Provision for credit losses was $3.1 billion, which included net charge-offs of $2.1 billion and a net reserve build of $1.0 billion. The reserve build was concentrated in the card portfolio, where we continue to see normalization toward pre-pandemic levels. The net charge-off rate in card was 3.5 percent, up from 2.6 percent a year ago. I want to emphasize that this is normalization, not deterioration. We are returning to levels consistent with 2018 and 2019, and our through-the-cycle loss models anticipated this trajectory.

The CET1 ratio was 15.3 percent under the standardized approach, well above our current regulatory requirement of 11.9 percent. We repurchased $6.0 billion of common stock during the quarter. The expense ratio was 55 percent, and we continue to invest in technology and controls, with technology spend on track for approximately $16 billion for the full year.

Looking at our forward outlook, we expect fourth quarter expenses to be approximately $22.5 billion. We remain committed to our medium-term ROTCE target of 17 percent through the cycle, though we acknowledge that the current rate environment and elevated capital levels create headwinds to achieving that consistently.

Operator: We will now open the line for questions.

Analyst, Barclays: Jeremy, on the NII trajectory into 2025, what rate assumptions are embedded in that $87 to $88 billion guide? And how sensitive is that to the pace of cuts versus terminal rate?

Jeremy Barnum: Our 2025 NII guide assumes roughly 150 basis points of cumulative cuts from the peak, with the terminal fed funds rate settling around 3.75 to 4 percent by mid-2025. Sensitivity is asymmetric. If cuts are slower, that is modestly positive for NII in the near term but could weigh on loan demand. If cuts are faster or deeper, we would see more pressure on deposit repricing, though volumes could pick up. The key variable is the slope of the curve and deposit beta behavior, which remains uncertain.

Analyst, Evercore ISI: Jamie, can you give us your view on the commercial real estate portfolio? Are you seeing stabilization in office valuations, and what is your reserve coverage there?

Jamie Dimon: Commercial real estate, particularly office, remains a challenged sector. We have been proactive in marking our exposures and building reserves. Our direct CRE exposure is well-managed and represents a relatively small portion of total loans. Office specifically is about $12 billion in outstanding. We have reserved against the weaker credits and are working through maturities on a case-by-case basis. I would say we are past the peak of concern, but there will be more losses to come, particularly in secondary and tertiary markets where return-to-office rates remain low.

Analyst, Wells Fargo: On capital return, given the significant excess capital position, should we expect buyback levels to remain elevated? And any update on the Basel III endgame timeline?

Jeremy Barnum: We have substantial excess capital, and our preference is to deploy it organically through lending and investment. Where we cannot, we will return it to shareholders. We increased the buyback this quarter and expect to maintain an elevated pace. On Basel III endgame, the reproposal is still working through the process, and we expect final rules to be somewhat less punitive than the original proposal, but timing remains uncertain.

JNJ.text:
Operator: Good morning, and welcome to the Johnson and Johnson Third Quarter 2024 Earnings Conference Call. I would now like to turn the call over to Joaquin Duato, Chairman and CEO.

Joaquin Duato, CEO: Thank you, and good morning. Johnson and Johnson delivered a strong third quarter with total sales of $22.5 billion, representing operational growth of 5.2 percent. These results demonstrate the strength of our focused portfolio following the Kenvue separation. Our two segments, Innovative Medicine and MedTech, are both performing well and positioned for durable growth.

Innovative Medicine generated sales of $14.6 billion, up 5.9 percent operationally. Our key growth drivers continued to perform. DARZALEX achieved global sales of $3.0 billion, up 19 percent, maintaining its leadership in multiple myeloma. TREMFYA delivered $1.1 billion, up 22 percent, as we expanded into inflammatory bowel disease indications. ERLEADA reached $710 million, up 18 percent in prostate cancer. We are particularly pleased with the trajectory of our recently launched products. TECVAYLI and TALVEY, our bispecific antibodies in multiple myeloma, generated combined sales of $530 million and are demonstrating strong clinical uptake.

However, I want to acknowledge the biosimilar headwinds we face. STELARA, which generated $2.5 billion this quarter, is now subject to biosimilar competition in the US, and we expect meaningful erosion beginning in Q4 2024 and accelerating through 2025. This is a significant near-term headwind, but we have been planning for it and our pipeline is designed to more than offset the impact over time.

MedTech sales were $7.9 billion, up 4.0 percent operationally. Our Cardiovascular segment, including our electrophysiology franchise, delivered strong growth of 7 percent. The PulseSelect pulsed field ablation platform continues its launch trajectory, and surgeon feedback has been very positive. Orthopedics grew 2 percent, with VELYS robotic-assisted surgery driving procedure growth in knees. Vision was approximately flat, as contact lens volumes were offset by competitive pricing pressure.

Joe Wolk, CFO: Thank you, Joaquin. Third quarter adjusted EPS was $2.42, up 8.6 percent year over year. Adjusted operating margin was 35.4 percent, reflecting disciplined cost management and favorable mix. R&D investment was $3.6 billion for the quarter, or approximately 16 percent of sales, as we continue to advance our pipeline of over 100 programs in development.

Free cash flow was $5.1 billion for the quarter. We repurchased $2.0 billion of shares and paid $3.0 billion in dividends. Our balance sheet remains strong with a net debt to EBITDA ratio of 1.2 times.

We are raising our full-year 2024 guidance. We now expect operational sales growth of 5.5 to 6.0 percent, up from 5.0 to 5.5 percent previously. Adjusted EPS guidance is updated to $9.86 to $9.96, from $9.70 to $9.90. For 2025, we want to be transparent that the STELARA biosimilar impact will be a significant factor. We estimate $4 to $5 billion of STELARA US revenue is at risk over 2025, and while our growth portfolio is robust, investors should expect a transition year.

Operator: We will now open the call for questions.

Analyst, JPMorgan: Joaquin, on the STELARA erosion curve, can you provide more specificity on how you expect the ramp to play out quarter by quarter in 2025? And what gives you confidence that TREMFYA can absorb share in IBD specifically?

Joaquin Duato: The erosion profile for STELARA will depend on the number and timing of biosimilar entrants. We expect initial price competition to be modest in Q4 2024, with more meaningful volume erosion beginning in the first half of 2025. By the second half, we could see 40 to 50 percent volume erosion in the US market. Regarding TREMFYA in IBD, the data from our GRAVITI and QUASAR Phase 3 programs are very compelling. TREMFYA showed differentiated efficacy in Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis, and we believe the IL-23 mechanism has advantages that physicians are increasingly recognizing. We are confident TREMFYA will become a multi-billion dollar franchise in its own right.

Analyst, Citi: Joe, on the MedTech margin trajectory, you have been investing heavily in robotics and electrophysiology platforms. When should we expect these investments to inflect toward margin accretion?

Joe Wolk: MedTech margins are currently around 28 percent on an adjusted basis. The investments in VELYS and PulseSelect are still in the early commercialization phase, which means higher upfront costs for sales force expansion, training, and capital placement. We expect margin improvement to begin in late 2025 as procedure volumes scale and we leverage the installed base. Our medium-term target for MedTech remains a 30 percent plus adjusted operating margin.

Analyst, Bernstein: Can you update us on the talc litigation and any potential resolution path?

Joaquin Duato: We remain committed to resolving the talc litigation in a manner that is fair to claimants and reasonable for the company. We continue to believe that our talc products are safe, as supported by decades of scientific evidence. We are exploring all avenues for resolution, including a potential third attempt at a prepackaged bankruptcy plan for LTL Management. We have set aside $10.5 billion in reserves, which we believe is adequate, but the ultimate outcome remains uncertain and subject to court proceedings.

XOM.text:
Operator: Good morning, and welcome to the ExxonMobil Third Quarter 2024 Earnings Conference Call. I would now like to turn the call over to Darren Woods, Chairman and CEO.

Darren Woods, CEO: Thank you, and good morning. ExxonMobil delivered third quarter earnings of $8.6 billion, or $2.01 per share, on revenue of $90.0 billion. While earnings were down from the exceptional levels of 2022 and early 2023, these results demonstrate the structural improvements we have made to our portfolio. We are generating strong returns at mid-cycle commodity prices, which is a direct result of the strategic investments we have been making over the past five years.

Upstream earnings were $6.2 billion on production of 4.6 million barrels of oil equivalent per day, a record for the company and up 24 percent year over year, driven by the Pioneer Natural Resources acquisition and strong organic growth in the Permian Basin and Guyana. In the Permian, we produced 1.4 million barrels of oil equivalent per day, with unit costs continuing to decline as we apply technology-driven efficiencies and our cube development approach. In Guyana, we are now producing approximately 640,000 barrels per day from three FPSOs, with the Yellowtail project ramping on schedule. We sanctioned the sixth development, Whiptail, during the quarter, which will bring gross capacity to over 1.3 million barrels per day by 2027.

Energy Products, our downstream segment, delivered earnings of $1.3 billion. Refining margins narrowed during the quarter as global refining capacity additions in the Middle East and Asia began to weigh on crack spreads. Our integrated refining and chemical operations in the US Gulf Coast partially offset this, with Beaumont running at record throughput. However, I want to be straightforward that downstream earnings are likely to face continued pressure into 2025 as new capacity comes online.

Chemical Products earned $893 million, up 35 percent year over year as polyethylene margins improved and our performance products portfolio, including Exceed and Enable resins, continued to command premium pricing. The integration of Denbury's CO2 pipeline network is also progressing, supporting our low-carbon solutions business.

Kathy Mikells, CFO: Thank you, Darren. Cash flow from operations was $17.6 billion for the quarter. Capital expenditures were $6.3 billion, bringing the year-to-date total to $19.2 billion, on track with our full-year guidance of $25 to $27 billion. Free cash flow was $11.3 billion, and we returned $9.8 billion to shareholders through $4.3 billion in dividends and $5.5 billion in share repurchases.

Our balance sheet is in excellent shape, with a net debt to capital ratio of 5 percent. We expect fourth quarter production to be in the range of 4.6 to 4.7 million barrels of oil equivalent per day, with some seasonal maintenance downtime offsetting continued Permian and Guyana growth.

For 2025, we are maintaining our capital spending guidance of $27 to $29 billion, with approximately 40 percent directed toward the Permian Basin and Guyana. We expect production to reach approximately 5.0 million barrels of oil equivalent per day by the end of 2025. On cost, we remain on track to deliver $15 billion of structural cost savings by 2027 relative to our 2019 baseline.

Operator: We will now begin the question-and-answer session.

Analyst, Truist: Darren, on the Permian specifically, how should we think about the trajectory of well productivity and cost per lateral foot as you integrate the Pioneer acreage? Are you seeing the synergies you expected?

Darren Woods: The Pioneer integration is going very well. We have now taken over operatorship of the majority of the Pioneer acreage and are applying our cube development and advanced completion techniques. Early results are encouraging. We are seeing 10 to 15 percent improvements in lateral productivity on wells where we have applied our technology, and drilling costs per lateral foot are trending down about 8 percent. The synergy target of $2 billion by 2026 remains on track, and frankly, I think there may be upside as we identify additional logistics and procurement efficiencies. The cultural integration has gone smoother than expected.

Analyst, RBC Capital Markets: Kathy, on capital allocation, with free cash flow running at this level and the balance sheet essentially unleveraged, is there appetite for additional large-scale M&A, or should we expect the current buyback pace to continue?

Kathy Mikells: Our priority framework is clear: invest in high-return projects first, maintain and grow the dividend, and return excess cash through buybacks. We are currently in a period of elevated capital spending as we develop our Permian and Guyana positions, and we want to maintain balance sheet flexibility. I would not rule out bolt-on acquisitions where they make strategic sense, but we are not actively pursuing another large-scale transaction. We are comfortable sustaining the current buyback trajectory of roughly $20 billion per year as long as the cash flow supports it.

Analyst, UBS: On the refining side, can you size the margin headwind you expect from new global capacity in 2025? And are you considering rationalizing any of your refining footprint?

Darren Woods: Global refining additions, primarily in the Middle East and China, will add approximately 2 to 3 million barrels per day of capacity over the next two years. That is meaningful and will pressure margins, particularly for less complex refiners. Our advantage is that our Gulf Coast facilities are among the most complex in the world, and we can process heavy sour crudes at significant discounts. We expect our capture rates to remain strong on a relative basis, even if absolute margins compress by 20 to 30 percent from current levels. We continuously evaluate our portfolio and have already exited or converted less competitive assets, including the Slagen refinery. No further major rationalizations are planned at this time.

WMT.text:
Operator: Good morning, and welcome to the Walmart Inc. Third Quarter Fiscal Year 2025 Earnings Conference Call. I would now like to turn the call over to Doug McMillon, President and CEO.

Doug McMillon, CEO: Thank you, and good morning, everyone. We had another strong quarter, with consolidated revenue of $169.6 billion, up 5.5 percent year over year, or 5.2 percent in constant currency. Our strategy is working. We are growing sales, expanding margins, and gaining market share across the business. What is particularly encouraging is the breadth of our momentum. We are seeing strength in grocery, general merchandise, and e-commerce, across both Walmart US and our international markets.

Walmart US had an excellent quarter with comp sales growth of 5.3 percent, marking the eighth consecutive quarter of comps above 4 percent. Transactions were up 3.1 percent and ticket was up 2.2 percent, so this is real traffic-driven growth, not just inflation. Grocery comps were up 5.6 percent, and we continue to gain share in food, including with higher-income households earning over $100,000, which now represent a growing portion of our customer base. This demographic shift is structural, not temporary.

General merchandise comps were positive for the first time in several quarters, up 0.8 percent, which is a significant milestone. Categories like home, toys, and hardlines are showing improvement, and we are benefiting from the rollout of our redesigned store layouts and improved assortment through our marketplace platform. E-commerce sales grew 22 percent in the US, with pickup and delivery contributing the majority of that growth. Our delivery capabilities now reach over 93 percent of US households with same-day delivery.

Sam's Club had a standout quarter with comp sales growth of 7.0 percent, excluding fuel. Membership income grew 14 percent, and member count reached an all-time high. We are seeing strong results from our Plus membership tier, and Sam's Club is benefiting from the renovation and remodel program we accelerated this year.

Internationally, Walmex continued to outperform with strong comps in Mexico. Flipkart in India delivered solid growth, though margins remain dilutive to the overall international segment. China was a challenge, with consumer sentiment remaining soft and deflationary conditions in certain categories.

John David Rainey, CFO: Thank you, Doug. Third quarter adjusted operating income was $6.7 billion, up 8.2 percent year over year. Adjusted operating margin expanded 10 basis points to 4.0 percent. This may seem modest, but it reflects meaningful progress given the inherent margin pressure from our growing e-commerce and advertising businesses, which carry different margin profiles.

Gross margin expanded 21 basis points, driven by lower markdowns, improved inventory management, and continued growth in our higher-margin alternative revenue streams. Walmart Connect, our advertising business, grew 26 percent in the US to $900 million. This is a high-margin, high-growth business that we believe can reach $5 billion in revenue within the next three to four years. Marketplace seller fees and fulfillment services also contributed to the margin expansion.

Inventory was well-managed, up only 0.6 percent year over year on a 5.5 percent sales increase. Free cash flow was $7.8 billion year to date, and we returned $4.9 billion to shareholders through dividends and buybacks. We ended the quarter with a debt to EBITDA ratio of 1.5 times.

We are raising our full-year guidance. We now expect net sales growth of 4.8 to 5.1 percent, up from 3.75 to 4.75 percent previously. Adjusted operating income growth is expected to be 8.5 to 9.25 percent, up from 6.5 to 8.0 percent. Adjusted EPS is now expected to be $2.42 to $2.47, up from $2.35 to $2.43.

For the fourth quarter, we expect US comps of 3 to 4 percent. We do expect some margin compression from holiday promotional activity, which is typical for Q4, and we are committed to maintaining our price leadership position during the holiday season.

Operator: We will now begin the question-and-answer session.

Analyst, Deutsche Bank: Doug, the higher-income customer cohort gains are notable. Can you characterize how sticky you believe this behavior is? If inflation moderates further, do you risk losing these customers back to traditional grocers?

Doug McMillon: We believe much of this shift is sticky because it is not just about price. These customers are discovering our pickup and delivery experience, our improved fresh assortment, and our private brand quality, and they are staying. Our customer satisfaction scores in grocery are at record levels. That said, we are not complacent. We know we need to keep improving the experience, particularly in areas like organic, specialty, and prepared foods, where higher-income customers have elevated expectations. The convenience proposition of getting everything in one trip or one delivery is powerful and hard to replicate.

Analyst, Guggenheim: John David, on Walmart Connect, the 26 percent growth is impressive but below the 30 plus percent we saw earlier. Is there a deceleration risk as you comp harder numbers, and how do you think about the margin contribution scaling?

John David Rainey: The slight deceleration is largely a function of the comp base. On a two-year stack, Walmart Connect growth is actually accelerating. We are still in the early innings of monetizing our first-party data and our in-store digital signage network. Advertiser count is growing, and average spend per advertiser is increasing. The margin profile is very attractive, with incremental margins well above 50 percent. We are also seeing increasing interest from non-endemic advertisers, which expands our addressable market significantly.

Analyst, Oppenheimer: On Walmart Plus, can you give us an update on membership trends and how you are thinking about the competitive positioning relative to Amazon Prime?

Doug McMillon: We do not disclose specific membership numbers, but I can tell you that Walmart Plus continues to grow at a strong clip. The value proposition has improved meaningfully with the addition of Paramount Plus streaming and fuel discounts. Our research shows that Walmart Plus members spend roughly 2.5 times more than non-members, so this is a powerful flywheel. We view our membership offering as complementary rather than directly competitive with Prime. Many of our members also have Prime memberships. Our edge is in grocery and the physical store network, which enables a fundamentally different fulfillment model.
